coredata.dc:title: Facile preparation of zinc oxide nanorods surrounded by graphene quantum dots both synthesized via separate pyrolysis procedures for photocatalyst application
coredata.dc:creator: Rahimi, Kourosh
Yazdani, Ahmad
Ahmadirad, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: A facile pyrolysis method was employed for separate synthesis of zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the structures were composited. The structures were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffractometry (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The structures were optically characterized by UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectrometry techniques, showing that the absorption of ZnO NRs increases in the visible region after being surrounded by GQDs. Optical bandgaps of ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs composite were estimated as 3.2 and 2.8eV, respectively. Based on PL analysis, it was deduced that the generated electron-hole recombination rate of ZnO NRs decreases after being surrounded by GQDs. Finally, it was shown that our GQDs, despite being relatively inefficient separately, significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity of ZnO on methylene blue mainly through decreasing the e-h recombination rate of ZnO NRs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Zinc oxide
Nanorod
Graphene quantum dot
Photocatalyst
Pyrolysis

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for providing financial support. The authors declare no competing interests.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: UV–vis absorption spectrum of the GQDs suspension, ten times diluted. A photograph of the diluted suspension is also shown. Tauc plot of the absorption curve is shown in the inset.

Normalized photoluminescence spectra of the as-synthesized GQDs suspension for various excitation wavelengths in the range of 370–530nm. The dependence of maximum PL intensities (left vertical axis) and wavelength of maximum PL intensity (right vertical axis) on excitation wavelength is shown in the inset. Photographs of the GQDs suspension under excitation of a UVA dark lamp (365nm) and a green laser pointer (532nm) are also shown.
FESEM images of the synthesized (a) ZnO NRs and (b) ZnO NRs/GQDs. (c) XRD pattern of the synthesized ZnO NRs. (d) TEM image of the synthesized ZnO NRs/GQDs.
FTIR spectra of ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs.
UV–vis spectra of ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs composite dispersed in water with the same concentration of 3mg/ml. Tauc plots of the absorption spectra are also shown in the inset.
PL spectra of the synthesized ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs with the same concentration of 3mg/ml, excited by 325nm.

Time variation of photocatalytic activities of GQDs, TiO2-P25, ZnO NRs, and ZnO NRs/GQDs. The initial values are less than 1, because we waited one hour in order to achieve an equilibrium between adsorption and desorption. Photographs of initial MB solution and MB solutions degraded by GQDs and ZnO/GQDs are also shown.
Stability of photocatalytic activity of ZnO NRs/GQDs for five successive cycles (Degradation percentage of methylene blue after 150min irradiation).
UV–vis absorption spectra of initial methylene blue solution and methylene blue solution after cycle 1.

head.ce:title: Facile preparation of zinc oxide nanorods surrounded by graphene quantum dots both synthesized via separate pyrolysis procedures for photocatalyst application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Condensed Matter Group, Department of Basic Sciences, Tarbiat Modares University, Jalal-Ale-Ahmad avenue, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile pyrolysis method was employed for separate synthesis of zinc oxide nanorods (ZnO NRs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the structures were composited. The structures were characterized by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffractometry (XRD), and Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy. The structures were optically characterized by UV–vis and photoluminescence (PL) spectrometry techniques, showing that the absorption of ZnO NRs increases in the visible region after being surrounded by GQDs. Optical bandgaps of ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs composite were estimated as 3.2 and 2.8eV, respectively. Based on PL analysis, it was deduced that the generated electron-hole recombination rate of ZnO NRs decreases after being surrounded by GQDs. Finally, it was shown that our GQDs, despite being relatively inefficient separately, significantly enhance the photocatalytic activity of ZnO on methylene blue mainly through decreasing the e-h recombination rate of ZnO NRs.

ce:section.ce:para: Dyes, such as methylene blue, are used in various industrial processing techniques, like in textiles and cosmetic, in order to color products. Therefore, industrial wastewater is full of dyes and pigments, which some of them are detrimental to environment and people. Thus, we have to apply some treatment techniques on the industrial wastewater before it can enter the nature again. A widely investigated technique is the use of photocatalysts. Photocatalysts are substances that can catalyze some reactions induced by light and have some applications such as water splitting and degradation of organic materials [1,2]. Semiconducting metal oxides (as TiO2 and ZnO) are widely used as photocatalysts, because they can generate electron-hole pairs, needed for redox reactions, upon absorption of sun light [3–8]. However, the semiconductors have such large bandgaps that they can harvest only a little part of sunlight, and the worse thing is that they also have a very high electron-hole recombination rate, which makes them very ineffective. Therefore, most researchers in the area focus on decreasing their bandgap energies and their electron-hole recombination rates. Furthermore, researchers are continuously searching for green, eco-friendly, and cost-effective methods for synthesizing the materials.

Zinc oxide (ZnO) is a wide- and direct-bandgap (∼3.3eV at room temperature) semiconductor with a very large excitonic binding energy (60 meV), allowing the design of devices for operating at temperatures above 300K [9]. ZnO is natively an n-type semiconductor due to its oxygen vacancies or zinc interstitials [10]. An interesting point about ZnO is that it can stably exist in various shapes such as rods. ZnO nanorods can be prepared by various methods including vapor-phase epitaxial growth [11], hydrothermal [12], solvothermal [13], pyrolysis [14], etc. Among the mentioned methods, thermal decomposition or pyrolysis is single-stage, straightforward and cost-effective.

There are several studies employing graphene, the honeycomb arrangement of carbon atoms, in order to resolve the mentioned problems of ZnO for photocatalytic applications [15]. Graphene, with its apparently very high theoretical specific surface area of 2630m2/g [16] and remarkable electron mobility of about 15000cm2/V*s at room temperature [17], can strongly capture electrons from the conduction band of ZnO; because it has a lower work function (−0.08V) than the potential of ZnO conduction band (−0.45V) versus normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) [18]. Recently, quantum dots of graphene (GQDs) have been used, as an alternative to graphene, in improving photocatalysts such as TiO2 and ZnO [19,20]. GQDs can be synthesized by various methods classified as top-down [21,22] and bottom-up [23–25] methods. Among the methods, pyrolysis of citric acid into graphene quantum dot is very straightforward and cost-effective and also green.

Due to the dominance of quantum confinement effect and also the effect of edges, a band gap is emerged in them leading to a strong photoluminescence. The emergence of n→π∗ transition in graphene quantum dots extends the absorption of graphene layers to the visible region of light for more harvesting of sunlight spectrum. Furthermore, GQDs can act as a barrier against the recombination of electrons and holes in order to remain reactive for longer times. There are few studies on improvement of photocatalytic activity of ZnO by the help of GQDs [26–28].

Here, we employed the facile, low-cost, and large-scalable method of pyrolysis to synthesize both ZnO NRs and GQDs and simply composited them by mild heating for promotion of bonds between ZnO NRs and GQDs. Our method yields photocatalyst in powder form that can be centrifugally recovered. Powder-form photocatalysts, due to their higher specific surface area than that of layer-form photocatalysts, can provide a far more photocatalytic activity. The less cost and the little time needed for our powder-form photocatalyst as well as its higher photocatalytic activity can compensate for its costly and time-consuming post-treatment recovery process. Methylene blue, which is widely used as a model dye to demonstrate the photocatalytic activity of potential photocatalysts, was also used here in order to study its degradation through decolorization in the presence of our prepared photocatalytic. Finally, the mechanism behind the photocatalytic activity was discussed, and the role of GQDs in the enhancement of ZnO NRs photocatalytic activity was investigated.
All precursors were of analytical grade prepared from Merck Co.

Graphene quantum dots were synthesized through pyrolysis of citric acid, as presented in [25]. At first, 2g of CA was placed into a glass tube, and it was submerged in an oil bath preheated to 200°C using a heater. The process of pyrolysis continued for 30min until it turned orange, indicating the formation of GQDs. After it was cooled to room temperature, 10mg/ml NaOH solution was added drop by drop into it under vigorous stirring until it reached to pH=7.0. The obtained aqueous solution of GQDs was maintained in a refrigerator in order to avoid aggregation.

ZnO nanorods in powder form were synthesized through pyrolysis of zinc acetate dihydrate, as presented in [29]. At first, 0.5g zinc acetate dihydrate was put into an alumina crucible, covered by an alumina lid, and then it was placed in an oven. The crucible was heated to 300°C and maintained for 3h. The obtained powder was washed several times with ethanol and DI water in order to remove undecomposed precursor and unwanted ions.

The as-synthesized ZnO NRs powder (20mg) was added into the as-synthesized GQDs suspension (1ml) and it was stirred for one hour to be properly mixed. Next, the mixture was dried at 50°C in a vacuum oven overnight. The obtained powder was heated at 150°C for one hour in order to promote the formation of bonds between ZnO NRs and GQDs. Because of the nature of pyrolysis method, temperatures above 200°C could change the structure, which impacts our desired photocatalytic activity. On the other hand, very low temperatures (below 100) could not provide a good adhering between the GQDs and the ZnO NRs. Therefore, by considering the reasons mentioned above, we selected 150°C for compositing in a mild condition. Next, the resulting powder was washed with DI water in order to remove GQDs that have not adhered on ZnO NRs.

An X-ray diffractometer (XRD, X’Pert MPD, Philips) with a Cu-Kα radiation source (λ=1.5418 Å) was employed in order to analyze the crystallographic structure of ZnO nanorods. A field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi S-4160) and a transmission electron microscope (TEM, CM30) was employed to observe the samples. Room-temperature photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the samples were taken by a PL spectroscopy instrument (Gilden pλotonics equipped with a xenon lamp). UV–vis spectra of the samples were recorded by a double-beam spectrophotometer (Unico, 4802). A FTIR spectrometer (Nicolet 680) was employed in order to analyze the functional groups of the samples (as pellets in KBr).

Degradation of 50ml of 2.5×10−5 M aqueous solution of methylene blue (MB) by our synthesized ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs composite powder was studied through monitoring decolorization. After adding the photocatalyst powder into the MB solution, it was maintained in the dark for one hour in order to reach an equilibrium between adsorption and desorption. The light source was a 250W metal halide lamp (Philips) above the beaker. The irradiance of the lamp was adjusted to ∼1000W/m2 by a lux meter by changing its distance in order to simulate the solar power. The temperature was maintained at room temperature (∼25°C) by a water circulator in order to eliminate thermal effects. Decolorization of the solution was measured every 30min by measuring the absorbance of the solution at the wavelength of 665nm, corresponding to its highest absorption peak. All photocatalyst experiments were performed three times, and the reported values are average values.

UV–vis absorption spectrophotometry was performed to analyze the characteristic peaks and bandgap of GQDs as an evidence of their formation. Fig. 1 shows the UV–vis spectrum of the GQDs suspension, prepared by pyrolysis of citric acid, ten times more diluted, in the wavelength range of 200–700nm. The solution was diluted in order to see the peaks clearer. As it is seen, there is almost no absorption in the visible region (400–700nm), in contrast to the absorption in the ultraviolet region, starting from the edge of the visible region. A strong, maximum peak is seen at 240nm, corresponding to the π→π∗ transition of aromatic sp2 clusters and a weaker shoulder is seen in the range 320–350nm, corresponding to the n→π∗ transition of CO bonds [25,30,31].

Bandgap of GQDs was determined from the Tauc relation hν =A(hν − Eg)n where h is the Plank constant, ν is the frequency of incident light, A is the absorption, Eg is the bandgap and n is a constant that depends on the nature (direct or indirect) of the bandgap. Tauc plot ((αhν)2 versus hν for direct bandgap materials) of the UV–vis absorption spectrum is also shown in the inset of Fig. 1. The intercept of a straight line along the linear part of the curve with the hν axis indicates the bandgap, here ∼3.3eV. As it is known, graphene is theoretically a zero bandgap semiconductor [32]. However, when the lateral size of graphene is such little that quantum confinement effects dominate, a bandgap is opened that depends on the size. The more little the lateral size, the wider the bandgap [33].

Photoluminescence spectroscopy was performed in order to analyze the characteristic emission of GQDs as another evidence of their formation. Fig. 2 shows the normalized photoluminescence spectra of the as-synthesized GQDs for various excitation wavelengths. The GQDs suspension emits a greenish blue light (477nm) when excited with 370nm wavelength (see the photograph of the GQDs suspension excited by a UVA dark lamp with the peak wavelength of 365nm in Fig. 2). The wavelength corresponding to the peak PL increases from 477 to 570nm as the excitation wavelength increases from 370 to 540nm (see the photograph of the GQDs suspension excited by a green laser pointer with the wavelength of 532nm in Fig. 2). As it is seen, the GQDs exhibit an excitation-dependent photoluminescence, which can be attributed to the effects of both sp2 of various sizes and different emission sites of each sp2 cluster [25]. Dependence of maximum PL intensities and wavelengths on excitation wavelength is also shown in the inset of Fig. 2. As it is seen, the excitation wavelength of 420nm yields the maximum emission.

FESEM images were taken in order to observe the morphology of the samples. Fig. 3 (a, b) shows the FESEM images of the prepared ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs powder. As can be seen, both samples consist mainly of nanorods of various lengths in the range of 200–1000nm. The various lengths of the nanorods are due to the nature of thermal decomposition method as a somehow bottom-up method. The mean diameters of the ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs were calculated as ∼70nm and ∼90nm, respectively. The increased diameter in the case of ZnO NRs/GQDs is due to the presence of GQDs around the ZnO NRs that can be seen as small particles adhered on the ZnO NRs in Fig. 3.

XRD pattern of the ZnO NRs was analyzed in order to characterize the crystal structure of the ZnO NRs. Fig. 3(c) shows the XRD pattern of the synthesized ZnO NRs, which is similar to the pattern in [29]. The diffraction peaks are sharp indicating a high crystallinity. All peaks are matched with the JCPDS Card No. 01-079-0208, indicating that the obtained phase is hexagonal wurtzite with lattice constants of a=3.26 Å and c=5.21 Å. No other peak is observed, indicating that there is no secondary phase and thus the sample does not contain any significant impurity.

TEM image was taken to observe the GQDs around ZnO NRs. Fig. 3(d) shows the TEM image of the ZnO NRs/GQDs. GQDs can be seen around the ZnO NRs. As it seen, GQDs compactly surrounded the ZnO NR, similar to Fig. 3(b). Based on the TEM image, the rod diameter is 125nm, and the mean diameter of GQDs is ∼20nm.

FTIR analysis was performed in order to confirm the presence of GQDs in the prepared composite and determine the existing functional groups. Fig. 4 shows the FTIR spectra of the bare ZnO NRs powder and the composite ZnO NRs/GQDs powder over the wavenumbers of 300–3800cm−1. Three common bands can be seen in both spectra: (1) a broad, intense band around 3356cm−1 corresponding to the stretching mode of OH bonds of hydroxyl groups on the surface of ZnO [34], (2) a weak band around 918cm−1 that may be attributed to the stretching vibration of CH, suggesting the presence of some incompletely carbonized CA in the composite [25], and (3) an intense band around 486cm−1 and 521cm−1 for ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs, respectively, which is related to the stretching vibration of Zn–O bonds in ZnO molecules [35,36]. The shift that is seen in ZnO peak of the ZnO NRs/GQDs as compared to that in ZnO NRs may indicate some interactions between ZnO and functional groups of GQDs. Some additional bands can be seen in the FTIR spectrum of the prepared composite, indicating the presence of GQDs in the structure. The shoulder about 2970cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CH bonds [25]. The strong band observed at 1585cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CC bonds in sp2 carbon, indicating the presence of graphitic structures [37]. The peak at 1400cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO bonds.

Fig. 5 shows the UV–vis spectra of the prepared ZnO NRs powder and ZnO NRs/GQDs powder with the same concentration of 3mg/ml in DI water. The same concentration was chosen in order to be able to compare their absorption. The spectra are comparable to those in [38]. A strong band-edge absorption peak can be observed in the UV–vis spectrum of the bare ZnO NRs at 370nm (=3.35eV), which shows a blue shift relative to the corresponding value of the bulk ZnO reported at 380nm [39]. The blue shift is due to the transition from macroscale to nanoscale, resulting in the domination of quantum confinement effect. In the case of ZnO NRs/GQDs, the band edge peak is slightly red shifted (376nm=3.30eV). Although the peak intensity and UV absorption are lower for the ZnO NRs/GQDs than those of the bare ZnO NRs, the absorption in the visible region is significantly higher over the entire visible range, which is very suitable for applications that harvest solar light.

Bandgap energies of the samples were estimated from the above-discussed Tauc plot, presented in the inset of Fig. 5. The value of n was set ½, because ZnO shows a direct transition between its valence and conduction bands. Therefore, the optical direct bandgap energies of the prepared ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs were calculated 3.2 and 2.8eV, respectively. The relatively large decrease in the bandgap is suitable for photocatalyst application under visible light to harvest solar light. The increased absorption and the decreased bandgap of ZnO NRs after incorporation of GQDs are another evidence of the formation of bonds between ZnO NRs and GQDs.

Analysis of photoluminescence spectra can help in understanding the recombination rate of the photogenerated e-h pairs, which drastically influences on photocatalyst activities. Fig. 6 compares the room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the synthesized ZnO NRs and the prepared ZnO NRs/GQDs composite, both with the same concentration of 3mg/ml in DI water, excited by the wavelength of 325nm. Again as in UV–vis spectrophotometry, the same concentration allows us to have a comparable insight into the PL spectra. As it is seen, an intensive ultraviolet band with a peak at 378nm is observed for the bare ZnO NRs that can be attributed to recombination of free excitons, leading to an emission, and it is the characteristic band-to-band or near band-to-band emission of ZnO. Moreover, a weaker broad band in the green region around 508nm is observed that can be attributed to structural defects such as impurities and singly ionized vacancies, i.e., due to the recombination of a photogenerated hole with a singly ionized charged state [40–42]. In the spectrum related to the prepared ZnO NRs/GQDs composite, photoluminescence is very weaker. The green peak is completely disappeared, and the UV emission band becomes broader and very weaker. The large decrease in the PL after incorporation of GQDs indicates a large decrease in the radiative recombination rate of e-h pairs. In fact, the GQDs on the ZnO NRs can capture the photogenerated electrons for some time, preventing them to be recombined with their hole counterparts, and the separated electrons or holes can participate in photocatalytic reactions [4]. Electron-hole pairs that are not trapped are usually recombined to each other on the order of several picoseconds.

As it was mentioned, UV–vis absorption and photoluminescence spectra showed the potential of the prepared ZnO NRs/GQDs composite for photocatalyst applications, because of (1) its higher absorption in the visible region of light and (2) its decreased photoluminescence that indicates its lower recombination rate of electron-hole pairs. Considering the potential capabilities mentioned above, methylene blue dye degradation was analyzed as a standard measure of the photocatalytic activities of the prepared ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/GQDs composite.

It is usually believed that the pseudo-first-order Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics [43] governs the degradation of organic dyes, such as methylene blue, as Ct =C0e−kt or −Ln(Ct/C0)=kt, where C0 is the initial concentration of methylene blue, Ct is the concentration of MB after being irradiated for time t, and k is the rate constant of the degradation reaction. If we plot the −Ln(Ct/C0) values versus time, the slope of a line fitted to the data yields the k value.

Fig. 7 shows the time variation of Ct/C0 of methylene blue while being photocatalytically degraded in the presence of 1ml of the as-synthesized GQDs, 20mg TiO2-P25 (as a benchmark for analysis of photocatalysts’ activity), 20mg ZnO NRs, and 20mg ZnO NRs/GQDs. We performed the photocatalyst experiment with 1ml GQDs in order to have comparable results. Because, as it was said in the experimental section, we composited 20mg of ZnO NRs with 1ml of the as-synthesized GQDs. As it is seen in Fig. 7, the photocatalytic activity of the bare ZnO NRs is weaker than that of the standard photocatalyst TiO2-P25, and it did not even achieve a C/C0 value lower than 0.1 after being irradiated for 300min. However, the prepared ZnO NRs/GQDs composite shows a significantly higher photocatalytic activity than that of the bare ZnO NRs and even than that of TiO2-P25. Moreover, the plots of their −Ln(Ct/C0) versus time are shown in the inset of Fig. 7. All the three photocatalyst samples exhibit a linear behavior obeying the pseudo-first-order Langmuir–Hinshelwood kinetics, and the k (rate constant) values are shown in the inset of Fig. 7 along their corresponding lines. According to the k values, the ZnO NRs/GQDs sample exhibits a ∼2.6 and 1.5 times higher photocatalytic activity than ZnO NRs and TiO2-P25, respectively. The enhancements are comparable to the values reported in other work. For example, by using ZnO NWs/GQDs, Ebrahimi et al. [26] achieved a maximum 2.8 times higher photocatalytic activity than that of bare ZnO NWs on methylene blue. In another work, by using ZnO/carbon quantum dots, Li et al. [27] achieved a∼3 times higher photocatalytic activity than that of bare ZnO on RhB.

In order to examine recyclability of ZnO NRs/GQDs photocatalytic activity in degradation of methylene blue, the photocatalytic activity was measured for five successive cycles, each after 150min irradiation. Degradation percentages ((1-C/C0)×100) of every cycle are plotted in Fig. 8 . As can be seen, a decrease occurs in the cycle 2 as compared to the cycle 1; however, it achieves a stable degradation percentage after that. The decrease could be due to the separation of some of the GQDs from the ZnO NRs, which results in loss of some part of the photocatalytic activity. Because, as it was seen in Fig. 7, our GQDs, alone, do not have a significant photocatalytic activity. In order to verify the assumption that some amount of GQDs is peeled off the ZnO NRs, Fig. 9 shows the UV–vis spectra of initial methylene blue solution and methylene blue solution after cycle 1 of Fig. 8. As it is seen, UV absorption of methylene blue solution after cycle 1 of degradation increases despite the overall decrease of absorption of MB solution. The observation could prove that some amount of GQDs have not been well-adhered on the ZnO NRs and peeled off during the test. Possibly, if the temperature used for surrounding ZnO NRs by GQDs (here 150°C) were increased more, the peeling off rate decreased; however, the increased temperature may result in a change in the structure, leading to a decrease in the photocatalytic activity. Some parts of the GQDs peeling off are inevitable in the first photocatalytic cycle; however, as can be seen in Fig. 8, after the cycle 1, the photocatalytic activity reaches a stable state.

Due to being a semiconductor, ZnO particles generate electron-hole pairs upon being impacted by photons with energies higher than their bandgap energy. The generated electrons and holes are either separated and transferred to the particle surfaces or recombined to each other. The electrons and holes that are able to be transferred to the surfaces can interact with H2O molecules, producing peroxide (O2−) and hydroxyl radical (OH) species, respectively, which are very receptive and reactive. The reactions are as follows:
ZnO+hν (>bandgap energy)→e− +h+

Oxidative reaction:
h+ +H2O→H+ + OH
2h+ +2H2O→2H+ +H2O2 →2H+ +2OH

Reductive reaction:
e− + O2 → O2 −
O2 − +2HO•+H+ → H2O2 +O2
HOOH→HO + OH

Hydroxyl radicals, which are produced in both oxidative and reductive reactions, are highly oxidative and non-selective and have a redox potential of +3.06V [44]. They participate in the degradation of methylene blue molecules into smaller fractions, ultimately into CO2 and H2O.

It has been showed that GQDs could be activated under blue light in order to kill microorganisms [45]. However, it has been explained in [46] that most GQDs exhibit absorption bands in the ultraviolet region and consequently show insignificant photocatalytic activities in the visible region. As it seen in Fig. 7, similarly, our synthesized graphene quantum dots do not show any significant photocatalytic activity. Therefore, the increased photocatalytic activity does not result from the separate role of GQDs. The increased photocatalytic activity after incorporation of GQDs onto the ZnO NRs can hence be classified into two main categories:
(1)
The significantly increased absorption in the visible region of light and the decreased bandgap energy from 3.2 to 2.8eV, leading to more harvesting of the sunlight spectrum. From Fig. 7, it is obvious that GQDs have not a good absorption in the visible region of light and mainly absorbs light in the ultraviolet region. Therefore, it can be said that our GQDs do not play a separate role in the increased absorption. The observation that our synthesized GQDs have not a significant absorption in the visible region, as compared to the UV region, could be somehow useful for the photocatalyst activity of ZnO. Because, the compact population of GQDs around ZnO NRs would act as a barrier against light if the GQDs had a significant absorption in the visible region.
(2)
The decreased recombination rate of the photogenerated electron-hole pairs. As it was discussed regarding the PL spectra, GQDs can largely decrease the PL of ZnO NRs, which means that GQDs can retard the recombination of electron and hole pairs in order to increase their chance to be separated from each other and participate in oxidative and reductive reactions.

ZnO NRs/GQDs composite was synthesized by a facile, cost-effective, and large-scalable method called pyrolysis. Based on the findings obtained from UV–vis absorption spectra and PL spectra, i.e., the increased visible absorption and decreased bandgap after incorporation of GQDs and the significantly decreased PL after incorporation of GQDs, we decided to make use of the emergent capabilities, thanks to GQDs, for photocatalytically degradation of methylene blue. The results indicated that the ZnO NRs/GQDs sample exhibits a ∼2.6 and 1.5 times higher photocatalytic activity than ZnO NRs and TiO2-P25, respectively. Finally, the mechanism of the increased photocatalytic activity was discussed and mainly attributed to the participation of GQDs in capturing the photogenerated electrons and holes, resulting in a decreased e-h recombination that is detrimental to photocatalysts, without having any significant separate role.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank Research Council of Tarbiat Modares University for providing financial support. The authors declare no competing interests.
